did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computer algorithms (specifically deep neural networks) from 23 teams in the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge to classify dermoscopy images of cutaneous melanoma, nevi, and seborrheic keratoses. The study evaluated the performance of these AI models against dermatologists and residents, demonstrating that deep neural networks could classify skin images with high accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes a cross-sectional study involving original experimental data collection and analysis. It utilized 150 dermoscopy images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge and evaluated the performance of computer algorithms against dermatologists and residents in an online reader study, reporting specific statistical results (e.g., AUC values, sensitivity, specificity, P-values).

title: Computer algorithms show potential for improving dermatologists’ accuracy to diagnose cutaneous melanoma: Results of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017
brief_summary: This cross-sectional study evaluated whether computer algorithms from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge could improve dermatologists' accuracy in diagnosing cutaneous melanoma. The researchers analyzed 150 dermoscopy images of melanomas, nevi, and seborrheic keratoses using results from 23 algorithm teams. Eight dermatologists and nine residents classified these images in an online reader study, providing confidence levels for their assessments. The top-ranked computer algorithm achieved a significantly higher area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (0.87) compared to dermatologists (0.74) and residents (0.66). Furthermore, integrating algorithm classifications into dermatologist evaluations with low confidence ratings increased sensitivity from 76.0% to 80.8%. The study concludes that deep neural networks can classify skin images with high accuracy and potentially enhance human diagnostic performance.

lesion_types_studied.details: 50 melanoma images used in the test dataset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge; primary diagnostic target.
50 nevus images used in the test dataset; described as a benign mimicker of melanoma.
50 seborrheic keratosis images used in the test dataset; described as a benign mimicker of melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of cutaneous melanoma, nevi, and seborrheic keratoses
all_task_types.details: The study evaluates computer algorithms and human readers in classifying dermoscopy images into discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. benign mimickers). The primary task is binary classification (melanoma detection) and multiclass classification (distinguishing between melanoma, nevi, and seborrheic keratoses), evidenced by reported sensitivity, specificity, and AUC metrics for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 Challenge Test Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the test dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 melanoma detection challenge, consisting of 150 dermoscopy images used for the cross-sectional study.

use.testing: Used as the input data for the online reader study where dermatologists and residents classified the images, and for evaluating the performance of 23 computer algorithm teams.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of the top-ranked computer algorithm against dermatologists and residents, and to compare specificity between the algorithm and dermatologists.

hypothesis_testing.test: Significance testing (P-values) comparing Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) and specificity
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) between the top-ranked computer algorithm and the dermatologists/residents; there is no difference in specificity between the algorithm and dermatologists.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The top-ranked computer algorithm has a higher AUC than dermatologists and residents; the algorithm has superior specificity compared to dermatologists.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The top-ranked computer algorithm's AUC (0.87) was significantly higher than that of dermatologists (0.74) and residents (0.66) with P < .001 for all comparisons. Additionally, the algorithm demonstrated superior specificity (85.0% vs. 72.6%) compared to dermatologists at the dermatologists' overall sensitivity level, with P = .001.

ISIC 2017 Challenge Test Dataset (150 im.metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)
ISIC 2017 Challenge Test Dataset (150 im.notes: The top-ranked computer algorithm's AUC was significantly higher than that of dermatologists and residents (P < .001 for all comparisons).

ISIC 2017 Challenge Test Dataset (Evalua.notes: Evaluated at the dermatologists' overall sensitivity of 76.0%. The algorithm had superior specificity compared to dermatologists (P = .001).

ISIC 2017 Challenge Test Dataset (Integr.notes: Imputation of computer algorithm classifications into dermatologist evaluations with low confidence ratings (26.6% of evaluations) increased dermatologist sensitivity from 76.0% to 80.8% and specificity from 72.6% to 72.8%.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep neural networks (specifically, the top-ranked algorithm from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge; specific model architecture name not reported in this text)

architecture_details.source: Results from 23 teams in the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Top-ranked computer algorithm from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge
Deep neural networks (from 23 teams in the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge)
conclusions: Deep neural networks can classify skin images of melanoma and its benign mimickers with high accuracy.
Computer algorithms have the potential to improve human diagnostic performance, specifically by increasing dermatologists' sensitivity when their confidence ratings are low.
The top-ranked computer algorithm achieved a significantly higher area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (0.87) compared to dermatologists (0.74) and residents (0.66).
The study was limited by an artificial setting that lacked the full spectrum of skin lesions and clinical metadata.
important_remarks: The study was limited by an artificial setting that lacked the full spectrum of skin lesions and clinical metadata.

The evaluation relied on a small, balanced test dataset of only 150 dermoscopy images (50 melanomas, 50 nevi, 50 seborrheic keratoses) from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 challenge.

The improvement in dermatologist sensitivity (from 76.0% to 80.8%) was achieved by integrating algorithm classifications only into evaluations where dermatologists had low confidence (26.6% of evaluations).
There are potential conflicts of interest to consider: Dr. Codella is an employee and stockholder of IBM, and Dr. Halpern is a consultant for several companies.
